input.background: Refer to Example 3.5. Recall that USA Today found that of all U.S. firms that use diversity training, 38% state that their primary reason for using it is to stay competitive.
input.question: What is the probability that in a sample of 10 firms that use diversity training, all 10 primarily use it to stay competitive?

output.answer:
To see how to compute the probability that 10 of 10 firms will give 'stay competitive' as their primary reason, first consider the event that 3 of 3 firms give 'stay competitive' as the primary reason. Using the notation defined earlier, we want to compute the probability of the intersection $C_1 \cap C_2 \cap C_3$. Again assuming independence of the classifications, we have

$$
P\left(C_1 \cap C_2 \cap C_3\right)=P\left(C_1\right) P\left(C_2\right) P\left(C_3\right)=(.38)(.38)(.38)=.054872
$$

Similar reasoning leads us to the conclusion that the intersection of 10 such events can be calculated as follows:

$$
P\left(C_1 \cap C_2 \cap C_3 \cap \ldots \cap C_{10}\right)=P\left(C_1\right) P\left(C_2\right) \ldots P\left(C_{10}\right)=(.38)^{10}=.0000628
$$

Thus, the probability that 10 of 10 firms all give 'stay competitive' as their primary reason for using diversity training is about 63 in 1 million, assuming the events (stated reasons for using diversity training) are independent.

meta_info.caption: Probability of Independent Events Occurring Simultaneously-Diversity Training Study
meta_info.section: 3.6 The Multiplicative Rule and Independent Events
